title: Scottish tablet
subtitle: The harder, grainier Scottish confection: denser than fudge, less sweet than toffee
excerpt: Forty-eight pieces of Scottish tablet: caster sugar, condensed milk, butter, and milk cooked to 118°C (two degrees above the fudge target) then beaten aggressively off the heat until the sugar begins to grain and the mixture firms. The result is denser, drier, and more granular than fudge.
sourceNotes: Scottish tablet is documented in Scottish domestic confectionery from the eighteenth century: a hard, grainy sugar confection made from sugar, cream or condensed milk, and butter, cooked to a higher temperature than English fudge and beaten until it grains. Meg Dods, The Cook and Housewife's Manual (1826), a Scottish domestic cookery text in the public domain, documents the tablet as a traditional Scottish sweetmeat: sugar, cream, and a little butter cooked to a firm ball, beaten as it cools until the mixture becomes opaque and sets hard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut pieces in a bag layered with baking paper.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 weeks. The texture improves after 24 hours as the graining continues to firm.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target: 118°C — two degrees above the fudge soft-ball point, which is what produces the firmer, grainier tablet texture

glossaryTerms.definition: The rapid crystallisation that occurs when beaten sugar confection transitions from a glossy, liquid state to an opaque, granular solid. For tablet, this is the goal: once the mixture is removed from the heat and beaten vigorously, the sucrose molecules begin to form small crystals that give tablet its characteristic dry, crumbly, grainy texture. The beating must continue until grain set begins : the mixture turns opaque and thickens suddenly then poured immediately into the tin before it sets in the saucepan.

content.text: Scottish tablet is not fudge. It is cooked to a higher temperature (118°C versus 116°C for fudge), beaten more aggressively off the heat, and the result is a firmer, drier, crumblier confection with a pronounced
. The texture is sandy and dissolves slowly on the tongue rather than melting smoothly. Two degrees makes the difference.

attrs.title: Hot sugar is extremely dangerous
attrs.body: Boiling sugar syrup reaches over 118°C and sticks to skin. Keep children away from the hob during cooking. Use a deep saucepan to prevent splashing. If any syrup splashes onto skin, run immediately under cold water for at least 10 minutes.

content.text: Grease and line a 20 × 30 cm tin. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{condensed-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{whole-milk}} in a large, heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Stir continuously until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Increase to medium heat and bring to a steady boil, stirring constantly to prevent the mixture catching on the base. Cook until the temperature reaches 118°C on a sugar thermometer — 20–25 minutes. The mixture will be deep golden.

Remove from the heat and stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 8–15 minutes. The mixture will be liquid and glossy at first, then begin to thicken, lighten in colour, and take on an opaque, sandy appearance — this is grain set beginning. The moment it thickens suddenly and becomes difficult to stir, pour it immediately into the prepared tin. Do not wait: it sets within seconds of grain set.
Smooth the surface quickly and score into 48 pieces while still warm. Leave to cool at room temperature for 2 hours until fully hard. Break along the score lines and store in an airtight tin.
